method3.generated_article:
==Volcano and caldera==
Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai is a mostly submarine volcano in the Tonga segment of the Tonga–Kermadec intra-oceanic arc, formed where the Pacific Plate subducts beneath the Indo-Australian Plate.[4] The volcano rises more than 1.8 km from the seafloor, extends about 20 km across, and has a summit caldera roughly 5 km in diameter.[4] Its eruptive products are andesitic, a composition typical of intra-oceanic volcanic arcs.[4]

The pre-2022 islands of Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai were the emergent northern and western remnants of the volcano’s caldera rim.[2][4] Geological observations described the islands as elongated and tangent to a circle centred on a rocky shoal about 3 km south of Hunga Tonga, the site of eruptions in 1912 and 1937.[2] Samples from Hunga Tonga showed alternating andesitic lava flows, scoria, lapilli and ash beds dipping gently away from that centre, supporting the interpretation that the islands were remnants of a large submarine caldera.[2] High-resolution multibeam sonar later identified a 4 by 2 km, 150 m deep depression at the inferred caldera location, consistent with collapse during an earlier explosive eruption.[2]

During the 2014–2015 eruption, a cone of tephra joined Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai, creating the single island known as Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai.[4][5] Before the January 2022 eruption, the joined island covered just over 1 km² and reached a maximum elevation of 149 m.[5] The 15 January 2022 eruption destroyed most of this land, leaving only small remnants of the former Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai islands above sea level.[4][5] The eruption has been described as a once-in-1,000-years event for the Hunga caldera and may rank as high as VEI 5, although more precise estimates of erupted volume are required to confirm that classification.[5]

==Geography==
The volcano is in the South Pacific within Tonga, about 30 km south of the submarine volcano Fonuafoʻou and about 65 km north of Tongatapu, Tonga’s main island.[4] It lies on the Tonga segment of the Tonga–Kermadec intra-oceanic arc, where the Pacific Plate is subducted beneath the Indo-Australian Plate.[5] The broader Tonga–Kermadec subduction system extends for about 2,550 km between New Zealand and Tonga, placing the volcano within a major southwest Pacific island-arc setting.[5]

The above-water land associated with the volcano was uninhabited and isolated from Tonga’s main population centres.[4] Satellite monitoring of the island’s landforms attracted scientific interest because its rapid erosion and geomorphic evolution provided an Earth analogue for interpreting similar volcanic features on Mars.[5] After the 2022 eruption, exposed land was limited to small fragments at the western and eastern margins of the former island area.[4]

The volcano’s geographic influence extends beyond its immediate edifice, as the 2022 eruption dispersed ash across the Haʻapai, Tongatapu and ʻEua island groups.[4] Reported ash thicknesses ranged from 5 to 50 mm across those groups, with about 20 to 30 mm reported on Tongatapu.[4] The same eruption generated tsunami waves that affected inhabited islands in Tonga and propagated across the Pacific to coastlines in South America, North America and northeast Asia.[4]

==Eruptions==
The climactic 2022 event began at about 5 p.m. local time on 15 January, when an underwater vent roughly 200 m below the ocean surface erupted explosively and injected gas, ash, aerosols and water vapour high into the atmosphere.[1][4] Satellite-era observations recorded a plume exceeding 30 km in height, with overshooting tops above 55 km, and surface-pressure analyses estimated the initial explosion released about 10–28 EJ of energy.[5] The event has been compared energetically with major historical eruptions, with the upper estimates approaching the scale of Krakatoa in 1883 and exceeding estimates for the 1991 eruption of Mount Pinatubo.[5]

The eruption was driven by intense interaction between magma and seawater, with magma temperatures commonly above 1,000 °C contrasting sharply with seawater near 20 °C.[3] NASA scientist James Garvin and colleagues described the event as unlike a standard Surtseyan eruption, because the volume of water involved was unusually large, and informally referred to it as an “ultra Surtseyan” eruption.[3] One proposed explanation is that failure of part of the caldera rim allowed large volumes of seawater to enter a hot magma chamber, although the absence of instruments on the volcano prevented direct confirmation of the sequence of events.[3][4]

The eruption generated destructive tsunami waves in Tonga, where run-up reached as high as 65 feet above sea level, hundreds of structures were destroyed, more than 1,500 people were displaced and four people died.[4] Tsunami waves also caused damage or other effects around the Pacific, including two deaths and an oil spill in Peru, as well as reported impacts in Fiji, Hawaii, Chile, California, Japan, Russia and New Zealand.[4] Post-eruption surveys found fine sandy mud and deep ash ripples up to 50 km from the volcano, with gouged valleys, large sediment piles and up to about 1⅔ cubic miles of displaced material recorded on the seafloor.[4] Tonga’s domestic internet cable, severed during the eruption, was found buried under about 100 feet of sediment and ash.[4]

The eruption injected an exceptional amount of water vapour into the stratosphere, increasing total stratospheric water content by about 10%, while also emitting sulfur dioxide that formed sulfate aerosols.[1][2] Because sulfate aerosols reflect sunlight and tend to cool the atmosphere, whereas stratospheric water vapour can contribute to warming, the eruption produced an unusual combination of climate forcings.[1][2] Subsequent studies found that the eruption did not cause the expected net global warming; instead, it produced a slight overall cooling effect from 2022 and reduced Southern Hemisphere temperatures by about 0.1 °C, while leaving indications of a very small warming influence in the Northern Hemisphere.[1] Scientists emphasized that the event demonstrated how shallow submarine eruptions can produce complex atmospheric effects and highlighted the importance of satellite monitoring of the stratosphere.[1][2]

==Footnotes==
The volcano is often abbreviated HTHH in scientific literature, and its vent has been described as lying at 20.536°S, 175.382°W in the Tonga–Kermadec volcanic arc, about 150 m below sea level.[1]

The 15 January 2022 eruption has been characterized as one of the largest volcanic events recorded with modern geophysical instruments, with an estimated volcanic explosivity index of about 6 and a plume reaching up to 57 km in altitude.[1]

The eruption covered an area at least 600 km in diameter with tephra and discharged about 2.9 × 10^15 g of basaltic-andesitic material, including ash and pumice, into the South Pacific Gyre.[1]

Contemporary reports and later reviews described the event as the largest eruption recorded globally in more than three decades and noted tsunami waves across the Pacific, including in Tonga, Japan, Australia, and the Pacific coasts of the Americas.[2][3]

One proposed trigger for the eruption’s explosivity is a submarine landslide that removed overlying rock, a mechanism compared with the 1980 eruption of Mount St. Helens and with the instability of emerging island volcanoes.[2]

Research on the tsunami has emphasized that the event has implications for interpreting historical and prehistoric Pacific tsunamis and for improving models of coupled atmospheric–oceanographic resonance.[3]

Studies of the eruption’s atmospheric effects found that its unprecedented injection of stratospheric water vapor differed from land-based eruptions, producing mid-stratospheric cooling of about 4 K for more than a year while warming the lower stratosphere by about 1–2 K.[4]

Other analyses concluded that the eruption did not produce the expected global warming and instead caused a net cooling of roughly 0.1 °C in the Southern Hemisphere by the end of 2022 and in 2023, partly because sulfate aerosols and ozone changes affected the radiative balance.[5]
